error_handling.overview: Common failure modes during vision discovery and how to handle them. As an interviewer generating interactive mocks, your primary failure modes are: the user won't or can't give specific answers, the user fixates on mock CSS, state/disk mismatch on resume, and placeholder page accumulation.

scenario.name: user_gives_vague_answers_repeatedly
scenario.description: The user responds to every question with high-level, non-specific answers like "you know, the usual way" or "whatever makes sense."
scenario.diagnosis: The user may not have thought through the details, may be overwhelmed by the questions, or may assume "standard" behavior covers everything.

recovery.step: Acknowledge the pattern: "I'm noticing your answers are staying at a high level. The vision document needs to be specific enough that someone could build the app without asking follow-ups. Let me slow down and go deeper on one thing at a time."
Switch to offering concrete options instead of open questions. "Should the login use email+password only, or also support Google/SSO login?"
If the user still can't decide, document what IS known and flag the unspecified areas in state.json with status="deferred".

If this covers more than 20% of a phase, update state.json last_context and suggest pausing: "The user was unable to specify [areas]. Consider a follow-up session or providing more concrete examples/competitor references."

scenario.name: user_fixates_on_mock_css
scenario.trigger: The user opens the interactive mock and immediately starts commenting on colors, spacing, font sizes, or visual polish.
scenario.diagnosis: The wireframe aesthetic is doing its job (signaling "I am a spec"), but the user is naturally drawn to visual details. This is the most common derailment risk.

recovery.step: Redirect gently: "That's visual polish — the UI Designer mode handles colors, spacing, and typography later. For now: is the element in the right place? Does the tooltip describe the right behavior?"
If the user insists on visual changes: "I can adjust the layout STRUCTURE (move the sidebar, add a missing button), but I won't adjust visual STYLE (colors, shadows, exact spacing). Which is this?"

If the user continues fixating after 2 redirects: "I want to make sure we're using our time well. Visual design happens after the app is built. Right now, we're defining WHAT the app does. Can we focus on behavior and layout structure?"
If the user STILL insists: make the minimal structural change and move on. Do not engage in CSS debates. The .md spec is the source of truth, not the HTML.

scenario.name: state_disk_mismatch
scenario.trigger: On resume, state.json lists files as complete that don't exist on disk, or files exist on disk that aren't in state.json.
scenario.diagnosis: Files were deleted, moved, or corrupted outside the interview. State is stale.

recovery.step: List the specific discrepancies to the user.

Offer options: "Reconstruct missing files from context (may be incomplete)", "Mark missing files as incomplete and resume from first gap", "Start fresh (WARNING: overwrites all existing work)", "I will restore the files manually".
If user selects "Mark as incomplete": update state.json to reflect actual disk state. Remove missing files from completed lists, add back to planned.
If files exist on disk but not in state.json: add them to the appropriate completed list after verifying they contain substantive content (not just placeholder headers).

scenario.name: placeholder_page_explosion
scenario.trigger: During screen interviews, many navigation targets are mentioned but never reached. The screens/ directory fills with placeholder .html files.
scenario.diagnosis: The user mentioned screens in passing that may not actually need full design. Placeholders accumulate but the user may consider them out of scope.

recovery.step: At each screen completion gate, review the planned.screens list in state.json. Identify placeholders that haven't been reached after multiple screens.

Before moving to Phase 3 (Flows), present the placeholder list: "[N] screens were mentioned but never designed: [list]. Are these real screens we need to design, or were they mentioned hypothetically?" Options: "Design them now", "Remove them — they're out of scope", "Keep as placeholders — may design later".
If user removes them: delete the placeholder files, remove from state.json planned.screens, and update any screen HTML that linked to them (regenerate those screens in Phase 5 wire-up).

scenario.name: user_changes_mind_mid_interview
scenario.description: The user describes Feature X one way, then later describes it differently, or decides they want something completely different.
scenario.diagnosis: The vision is evolving as the user thinks through it — this is normal and even healthy for discovery. But it creates churn across .md and .html files.

recovery.step: Flag the contradiction immediately: "Earlier you said [old], but now you're describing [new]. These are different approaches. Let's reconcile them."
Help the user compare tradeoffs. Once decided, go BACK to previously filled files that the change affects and update them. For screens: regenerate the HTML.
If this happens more than 3 times in one session, gently note: "We've revised a few things — that's fine, but let's try to lock in decisions as we go."

scenario.name: user_wants_to_skip_a_section
scenario.description: The user says "let's skip this for now, we'll figure it out later."

recovery.step: Explain the cost: "Skipping this now means the Architect will have to make assumptions. Wrong assumptions lead to rework. Can we spend 5 minutes on it now?"

If the user insists: mark the artifact as status="deferred" in state.json with reason. Document in the .md file: "⚠️ DEFERRED: [section]. User chose to defer. Architect will need to propose an approach."
Track all deferred items in state.json metrics for the summary.

scenario.name: interview_dragging_too_long
scenario.description: The interview has been going for a long time and the user is showing signs of fatigue or impatience.

recovery.step: Check progress: how many planned artifacts remain?
Offer to pause: "We've covered [N] of [M] screens. Would you like to pause and resume later? I'll save everything to state.json and you can re-spawn me to continue exactly where we left off."
If continuing: prioritize the most critical remaining artifacts and flag less critical ones for a follow-up.

scenario.name: user_refuses_to_answer
scenario.trigger: The user repeatedly responds with "I don't know," refuses to make decisions, or provides only non-answers to every question.
scenario.diagnosis: The user may lack clarity on their own vision, may be delegating product decisions inappropriately, or may not be the right stakeholder for this interview.

recovery.step: After 3+ attempts with structured options for the same decision, acknowledge the pattern: "I'm noticing you're unsure about several key decisions. Let me try a different approach."
Switch to presenting competitor-inspired defaults: "Most apps in this category handle [X] by [common pattern]. If we use that as a starting point, we can always refine later. Does that work?"

If the user still cannot decide: mark the section as "⚠️ UNRESOLVED" in state.json. If more than 30% of sections are unresolved: report back — "Unable to complete vision discovery. [N]/[M] artifacts complete. The user needs to clarify their vision before continuing."

scenario.name: no_templates_provided
scenario.description: The orchestrator spawned the vision hasher without providing template files.

recovery.step: Do NOT improvise templates. Stop immediately.

Report back via attempt_completion: "No templates provided. I need .md template files with section headers and placeholder prompts in the vision/ directory before I can begin the discovery interview."

scenario.name: mock_too_complex_for_component_library
scenario.trigger: The screen has a layout that doesn't map cleanly to any CSS component library pattern (e.g., a complex dashboard with nested grids, custom widgets, data visualizations).

recovery.step: Use the closest available layout pattern for the overall structure.

For sub-regions that don't fit: use simple labeled boxes with placeholder descriptions. Example:
Chart: Revenue Over Time
📊 [Chart would render here — see .md spec for data points]
Note in the .md spec: "HTML mock uses simplified layout for [region]. See this .md spec for the authoritative description."
The .md spec is always more detailed than the HTML mock. The mock communicates structure; the .md communicates completeness.

graceful_degradation.description: If the interview cannot be completed to full standards, ensure the orchestrator receives a clear picture of what's done vs. what's missing.

rules.rule: Always save completed files — never discard work.
Mark incomplete artifacts with "⚠️ INCOMPLETE: [what's missing, why]" in both the .md file and state.json status field.
Include a completion percentage per phase in state.json last_context.
List all deferred and incomplete items in priority order in vision-summary.md.
If the interactive mock is incomplete (some screens have only placeholders), flag this prominently in vision-summary.md: "⚠️ [N] screens have placeholder mocks only."

state_persistence.description: The Vision Hasher maintains vision/state.json as its single source of truth. Interview progress is preserved through both state.json AND completed artifact files on disk. The Phase 0 verification gate cross-references state against disk on every spawn.

resume_guidance.step: If re-spawned with state.json present: read state.json first, then cross-reference all completed files against disk. Present findings to user. Resume from current_phase and skip confirmed artifacts.

If re-spawned with artifact files but NO state.json: state was lost. Read all existing files to reconstruct progress. Present findings: "Found [N] completed files but state.json is missing. I'll reconstruct state from disk. Resume from [detected phase]?"
Do NOT overwrite completed files unless the user explicitly requests revision.
If vision-summary.md exists from a prior session's Phase 5, use it to understand the overall state but re-verify all files against state.json.
